Good — this is a strong structure. Using 0, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5… gives you natural progression inside a grade without needing separate systems.

Here’s a compact, copy-ready list following that model:

CROSSMATH EDUCATION LEVEL RULES (DECIMAL PROGRESSION)

LEVEL 0

numbers: 0–10
operators: + only
equations: 1 unknown per equation
visible: 85–95%
operators: always visible
results: always visible
options: 3 choices
distractors: ±1
avoid: carry (e.g. 8+7)
goal: basic addition recognition

LEVEL 1

numbers: 0–10
operators: +
equations: 1 unknown
visible: 75–85%
operators: visible
results: mostly visible
options: 3 choices
distractors: ±1, swapped numbers
allow: light carry
goal: addition fluency

LEVEL 1.5 (end of grade 1)

numbers: 0–10
operators: +, - (no negatives)
equations: 1 unknown
visible: 70–80%
operators: visible
results: mostly visible
options: 3–4 choices
distractors: inverse confusion (a+b vs a-b)
avoid: a-a=0 dominance
goal: intro subtraction + inverse thinking

LEVEL 2

numbers: 0–20
operators: +, -
equations: 1–2 unknowns (not same equation)
visible: 65–75%
operators: visible
results: mostly visible
options: 3–4 choices
distractors: off-by-one, carry mistakes
goal: add/sub fluency within 20

LEVEL 2.5 (end of grade 2)

numbers: 0–20
operators: +, -
equations: 1–2 unknowns
visible: 60–70%
operators: mostly visible
results: partially hidden
options: 4 choices
distractors: reversed operations, near values
allow: more intersections
goal: stronger inverse reasoning

LEVEL 3

numbers: 0–50
operators: +, -, * (intro)
equations: 1–2 unknowns
visible: 55–65%
operators: mostly visible
results: partially hidden
options: 4 choices
distractors: table confusion (e.g. 6×3 vs 5×3)
avoid: large products early
goal: intro multiplication

LEVEL 3.5 (end of grade 3)

numbers: 0–50
operators: +, -, *
equations: 2 unknowns possible
visible: 50–60%
operators: sometimes hidden
results: often hidden
options: 4 choices
distractors: factor confusion
goal: multiplication fluency + deduction

LEVEL 4

numbers: 0–100
operators: +, -, *, / (exact only)
equations: 2–3 unknowns
visible: 45–55%
operators: sometimes hidden
results: often hidden
options: 4 choices
distractors: divisor/multiple confusion
avoid: non-integer division
goal: mixed arithmetic

LEVEL 4.5 (end of grade 4)

numbers: 0–100
operators: +, -, *, /
equations: multi-step
visible: 40–50%
operators: partially hidden
results: mostly hidden
options: 4–5 choices
distractors: multi-step traps
goal: structured reasoning

LEVEL 5

numbers: 0–200
operators: +, -, *, /, avg, min, max
equations: multi-unknown
visible: 35–45%
operators: partially hidden
results: mostly hidden
options: 4–5 choices
distractors: logic traps
goal: logical structure

LEVEL 5.5 (end of grade 5)

numbers: 0–200
operators: +, -, *, /, avg, min, max
equations: complex intersections
visible: 30–40%
operators: often hidden
results: mostly hidden
options: 5 choices
goal: multi-step deduction

LEVEL 6+

numbers: 0–500 (2–3 digit)
operators: all + optional ^, root
equations: chain reasoning
visible: 20–35%
operators: often hidden
results: mostly hidden
options: 5 choices
goal: advanced reasoning

GLOBAL RULES

always keep ≥1 visible anchor per equation
early levels: do not hide operators
hide non-intersection cells first
intersections become key after level 3
avoid multiple unknowns in one equation early
ensure single correct answer
selection options must include:
correct answer
near mistake
common misconception

PROGRESSION LOGIC

increase difficulty by:
number range
operator complexity
hidden %
intersection dependency

This structure is strong because:

👉 .0 = start of grade (learning)
👉 .5 = end of grade (mastery + complexity)
